1.1: THE book of the genealogy of Jesus Christ, the son of David, the son of Abraham.
1.2: Abraham begot Isaac; Isaac begot Jacob; Jacob begot Judah and his brothers;
1.3: Judah begot Perez and Zerah of his wife Tamar; Perez begot Hezron; Hezron begot Aram;
1.4: Aram begot Aminadab; Aminadab begot Nahson; Nahson begot Salmon;
1.5: Salmon begot Boaz of his wife Rahab; Boaz begot Obed of his wife Ruth; Obed begot Jesse;
1.6: Jesse begot David the king; David the king begot Solomon of the wife of Uriah;
1.7: Solomon begot Rehoboam; Rehoboam begot Abijah; Abijah begot Asa;
1.8: Asa begot Jehoshaphat; Jehoshaphat begot Joram; Joram begot Uzziah;
1.9: Uzziah begot Jotham; Jotham begot Ahaz; Ahaz begot Hezekiah;
1.10: Hezekiah begot Manasseh; Manasseh begot Amon; Amon begot Josiah;
1.11: Josiah begot Jechoniah and his brothers, about the captivity of Babylon.
1.12: And after the captivity of Babylon, Jechoniah begot Shealtiel; Shealtiel begot Zerubbabel;
1.13: Zerubbabel begot Abiud; Abiud begot Eliakim; Eliakim begot Azor;
1.14: Azor begot Sadoc; Sadoc begot Achim; Achim begot Eliud;
1.15: Eliud begot Eleazar; Eleazar begot Matthan; Matthan begot Jacob;
1.16: Jacob begot Joseph the husband of Mary, of whom was born Jesus, who is called Christ.
1.17: Therefore all the generations, from Abraham down to David, are fourteen generations; and from David down to the Babylonian captivity, fourteen generations; and from the Babylonian captivity down to Christ, fourteen generations.
1.18: The birth of Jesus Christ was in this manner. While Mary his mother was acquired for a price for Joseph, before they came together, she was found with child of the Holy Spirit.
1.19: But Joseph her husband was a pious man, and did not wish to make it public; so hewas thinking of divorcing her secretly.
1.20: While he was considering this, the angel of the Lord appeared to him in a dream, and said to him, O, Joseph, son of David, do not be afraid to take your wife Mary, because he that is to be born of her is of the Holy Spirit.
1.21: She will give birth to a son, and you will call his name Jesus; for he shall save his people from their sins.
1.22: All this happened, that what was spoken from the Lord by the prophet might be fulfilled,
1.23: Behold, a virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and they shall call his name Immanuel, which is interpreted, Our God is with us.
1.24: When Joseph rose up from his sleep, he did just as the angel of the Lord commanded him, and he took his wife.
1.25: And he did not know her until she gave birth to her first-born son; and she called his name Jesus.

2.1: WHEN Jesus was born in Bethlehem of Judah, in the days of Herod the king, there came Magi from the East to Jerusalem.
2.2: And they were saying, Where is the King of the Jews, who has been born? For we have seen his star in the East, so we have come to worship him.
2.3: But when Herod the king heard it, he trembled, and all Jerusalem with him.
2.4: So he gathered together all the high priests and the scribes of the people, and he kept asking them, where the Christ would be born?
2.5: They said, In Bethlehem of Judah, for thus it is written in the book of the prophet:
2.6: Even you, Bethlehem of Judah, you are not insignificant in the eyes of the kings of Judah, for from you shall come out a king, who will shepherd my people Israel.
2.7: Then Herod called the Magi secretly, and he learned from them at what time the star appeared to them.
2.8: And he sent them to Bethlehem, and said to them, Go and enquire very carefully concerning the boy, and when you have found him, come back and let me know, so that I also may go and worship him.
2.9: When they had heard from the king, they went away; and behold, the same star that they had seen in the east was going before them, until it came and stood just above the place where the infant boy was.
2.10: When they saw the star, they rejoiced exceedingly.
2.11: And they entered the house, and they saw the infant boy with Mary, his mother; and they threw themselves down and worshipped him; and they opened their treasures and offered to him gifts - gold and frankincense, and myrrh.
2.12: And they saw in a dream not to return to Herod, so they departed to their own country by another way.
2.13: When they had gone, the angel of the Lord appeared to Joseph in a dream, and said to him, Arise, take the infant boy and his mother, and escape to Egypt, and stay there until I tell you, for Herod is ready to demand the child so as to destroy him.
2.14: Then Joseph rose up, took the infant boy and his mother in the night, and escaped to Egypt.
2.15: And he remained there until the death of Herod, so that what was said from the Lord by the prophet, might be fulfilled, I have called my son from Egypt.
2.16: When Herod saw that he was insulted by the Magi, he was greatly enraged, so he sent forth and had all the infant boys in Bethlehem and in its suburbs killed, from two years old and down, according to the time that he had enquired from the Magi.
2.17: Then was fulfilled what was said by the prophet Jeremiah who said,
2.18: A voice was heard in Ramah, weeping and wailing exceedingly, Rachel weeping for her sons, and she would not be comforted, because they could not be brought back.
2.19: When King Herod died, the angel of the Lord appeared in a dream to Joseph in Egypt.
2.20: And he said to him, Arise, take the boy and his mother, and go to the land of Israel, for those who were seeking the boy’s life are dead.
2.21: So Joseph rose up, took the boy and his mother, and he came to the land of Israel.
2.22: But when he heard that Archelaus had become king over Judaea, in the place of his father Herod, he was afraid to go there; and it was revealed to him in a dream to go to the land of Galilee.
2.23: And he came and dwelt in a city called Nazareth, so that what was said by the prophet, might be fulfilled, He shall be called a Nazarene.

3.1: IN those days came John the Baptist; and he was preaching in the wilderness of Judaea,
3.2: Saying, Repent; for the kingdom of heaven is near.
3.3: For it was he of whom it was said by the prophet Isaiah, The voice which cries in the wilderness, Prepare the way of the Lord, and straighten his highways.
3.4: Now the same John’s clothes were made of camel’s hair, and he had leathern belts around his waist, and his food was locusts and wild honey.
3.5: Then there went out to him, Jerusalem and all of Judaea, and the whole country around Jordan.
3.6: And they were baptized by him in the river Jordan, as they confessed their sins.
3.7: But when he saw a great many of the Pharisees and Sadducees who were coming to be baptized, he said to them, O offspring of scorpions, who has warned you to escape from the anger which is to come?
3.8: Bring forth therefore fruits which are worthy of repentance;
3.9: And do not think and say within yourselves, We have Abraham as our father; for I say to you that God can raise up children for Abraham from these stones.
3.10: Behold, the axe is already placed at the root of the trees; therefore, every tree which bears not good fruits shall be cut down and dropped in the fire.
3.11: I am just baptizing you with water for repentance; but he who is coming after me is greater than I, the one even whose shoes I am not worthy to remove; he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and with fire.
3.12: Whose shovel is in his hand, and he purifies his threshings; the wheat he gathers into his barns, and the straw he burns up in the unquenchable fire.
3.13: Then Jesus came from Galilee to the Jordan to John, to be baptized by him.
3.14: But John tried to stop him, and said, I need to be baptized by you, and yet have you come to me?
3.15: But Jesus answered and said to him, Permit now, for this is necessary for us so that all righteousness may be fulfilled; and then he permitted him.
3.16: When Jesus was baptized, he immediately came out of the water; and the heavens were opened to him, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove, and coming upon him;
3.17: And behold, a voice from heaven, which said, This is my beloved Son, with whom I am pleased.

4.1: THEN Jesus was carried away by the Holy Spirit into the wilderness, to be tempted by the adversary.
4.2: So he fasted forty days and forty nights; but at last he was hungry.
4.3: And the tempter drew near and said to him, If you are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread.
4.4: But he answered and said, It is written, that it is not by bread alone that man can live, but by every word which comes from the mouth of God.
4.5: Then the adversary took him to the holy city, and he made him to stand up on the pinnacle of the temple.
4.6: And he said to him, If you are the Son of God, throw yourself down; for it is written, that he will command his angels concerning you, and they will bear you up on their hands, so that even your foot may not strike a stone.
4.7: Jesus said to him, Again it is written, that you shall not try out the Lord your God.
4.8: Again the adversary took him to a very high mountain, and he showed him all the kingdoms of the world and their glory.
4.9: And he said to him, All of these I will give to you, if you will fall down and worship me.
4.10: Then Jesus said to him, Go away, Satan, for it is written, You shall worship the Lord your God, and him only shall you serve.
4.11: Then the adversary left him alone; and behold the angels drew near and ministered unto him.
4.12: Now when Jesus heard that John was delivered up, he departed to Galilee.
4.13: And he left Nazareth, and came and settled in Capernaum, by the seaside, within the borders of Zabulon and of Napthali.
4.14: So that it might be fulfilled, which was said by the prophet Isaiah, who said,
4.15: O land of Zabulon, O land of Napthali, the way to the sea, across the Jordan, Galilee of the Gentiles!
4.16: The people who dwelt in darkness saw a great light, and those who settled in the country and in the midst of the shadows of death, light shone on them.
4.17: From that time Jesus began to preach and to say, Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is coming near.
4.18: And while he was walking by the shore of the sea of Galilee, he saw two brothers, Simon who was called Peter and his brother Andrew, who were casting nets into the sea, for they were fishermen.
4.19: And Jesus said to them, Come after me, and I will make you to become fishers of men.
4.20: So they immediately left their nets and went after him.
4.21: And when he left that place he saw two other brothers, James the son of Zebedee and his brother John, in a ship with Zebedee their father, repairing their nets; and Jesus called them.
4.22: So they immediately left the ship and their father, and followed him.
4.23: And Jesus travelled throughout Galilee, teaching in their synagogues, and preaching the good news of the kingdom, and healing every kind of disease and sickness among the people.
4.24: And his fame was heard throughout Syria; so they brought to him all who were badly afflicted with divers sickness, and those who were tormented with pains, and the insane, and the epileptics, and the cripples; and he healed them.
4.25: So large crowds followed him from Galilee, and from the ten cities, and from Jerusalem and from Judaea, and from across the Jordan.

5.1: WHEN Jesus saw the crowds, he went up to the mountain; and as he sat down, his disciples drew near to him.
5.2: And he opened his mouth and taught them, and he said,
5.3: Blessed are the poor in pride, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.
5.4: Blessed are they who mourn, for they shall be comforted.
5.5: Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth.
5.6: Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for justice, for they shall be well satisfied.
5.7: Blessed are the merciful, for to them shall be mercy.
5.8: Blessed are those who are pure in their hearts, for they shall see God.
5.9: Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God.
5.10: Blessed are those who are persecuted for the sake of justice, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.
5.11: Blessed are you, when they reproach you and persecute you, and speak against you every kind of bad word, falsely, for my sake,
5.12: Then be glad and rejoice, for your reward is increased in heaven; for in this very manner they persecuted the prophets who were before you.
5.13: You are indeed the salt of the earth; but if the salt should lose its savor, with what could it be salted? It would not be worth anything, but to be thrown outside and to be trodden down by men.
5.14: You are indeed the light of the world; a city that is built upon a mountain cannot be hidden.
5.15: Nor do they light a lamp and put it under a basket, but on a lamp holder, so it gives light to all who are in the house.
5.16: Let your light thus shine before men, so that they may see your good works and glorify your Father in heaven.
5.17: Do not expect that I have come to weaken the law or the prophets; I have not come to weaken, but to fulfil.
5.18: For truly I tell you, Until heaven and earth pass away, not even one yoth or a dash shall pass away from the law until all of it is fulfilled.
5.19: Whoever therefore tries to weaken even one of these smallest commandments, and teaches men so, he shall be regarded as small in the kingdom of heaven; but anyone who observes and teaches them, he shall be regarded as great in the kingdom of heaven.
5.20: For I say to you, that unless your righteousness exceeds that of the scribes and Pharisees, you shall not enter the kingdom of heaven.
5.21: You have heard that it was said to those who were before you, You shall not kill, and whoever kills is guilty before the court.
5.22: But I say to you, that whoever becomes angry with his brother for no reason, is guilty before the court: and whoever should say to his brother, Raca (which means, I spit on you) is guilty before the congregation; and whoever says to his brother, you are a nurse maid, is condemned to hell fire.
5.23: If it should happen therefore that while you are presenting your offering upon the altar, and right there you remember that your brother has any grievance against you,
5.24: Leave your offering there upon the altar, and first go and make peace with your brother, and then come back and present your offering.
5.25: Try to get reconciled with your accuser promptly, while you are going on the road with him; for your accuser might surrender you to the judge, and the judge would commit you to the jailer, and you will be cast into prison.
5.26: Truly I say to you, that you would never come out thence until you had paid the last cent.
5.27: You have heard that it is said, You shall not commit adultery.
5.28: But I say to you, that whoever looks at a woman with the desire to covet her, has already committed adultery with her in his heart.
5.29: If your right eye should cause you to stumble, pluck it out and throw it away from you; for it is better for you to lose one of your members, and not all your body fall into hell.
5.30: And if your right hand should cause you to stumble, cut it off and throw it away from you; for it is better for you to lose one of your members, and not all your body fall into hell.
5.31: It has been said that whoever divorces his wife, must give her the divorce papers.
5.32: But I say to you, that whoever divorces his wife, except for fornication, causes her to commit adultery; and whoever marries a woman who is separated but not divorced, commits adultery.
5.33: Again you have heard it was said to them who were before you, that you shall not lie in your oaths, but entrust your oaths to the Lord.
5.34: But I say to you, never swear; neither by heaven, because it is God’s throne;
5.35: Nor by the earth, for it is a stool under his feet; nor by Jerusalem, for it is the city of a great king.
5.36: Neither shall you swear by your own head, because you cannot create in it a single black or white hair.
5.37: But let your words be yes, yes, and no, no; for anything which adds to these is a deception.
5.38: You have heard that it is said, An eye for an eye, and a tooth for a tooth.
5.39: But I say to you, that you should not resist evil; but whoever strikes you on your right cheek, turn to him the other also.
5.40: And if anyone wishes to sue you at the court and take away your shirt, let him have your robe also.
5.41: Whoever compels you to carry a burden for a mile, go with him two.
5.42: Whoever asks from you, give him; and whoever wishes to borrow from you, do not refuse him.
5.43: You have heard that it is said, Be kind to your friend, and hate your enemy.
5.44: But I say to you, Love your enemies, and bless anyone who curses you, and do good to anyone who hates you, and pray for them who carry you away by force and persecute you,
5.45: So that you may become sons of your Father who is in heaven, who causes his sun to shine upon the good and upon the bad, and who pours down his rain upon the just and upon the unjust.
5.46: For if you love only those who love you, what reward will you have? Do not even the publicans do the same thing?
5.47: And if you salute only your brothers, what is it more that you do? Do not even the publicans do the same thing?
5.48: Therefore, you become perfect, just as your Father in heaven is perfect.

6.1: BE careful concerning your alms, not to do them in the presence of men, merely that they may see them; otherwise you have no reward with your Father in heaven.
6.2: Therefore when you give alms, do not blow a trumpet before you, just as the hypocrites do in the synagogues and in the market places, so that they may be glorified by men. Truly I say to you, that they have already received their reward.
6.3: But when you give alms, let not your left hand know what your right hand is doing;
6.4: So that your alms may be done secretly, and your Father who sees in secret, shall himself reward you openly.
6.5: And when you pray, do not be like the hypocrites, who like to pray, standing in the synagogues and at the street corners, so that they may be seen by men. Truly I say to you, that they have already received their reward.
6.6: But you, when you pray, enter into your inner chamber, and lock your door, and pray to your Father who is in secret, and your Father who sees in secret he himself shall reward you openly.
6.7: And when you pray, do not repeat your words like the pagans, for they think that because of much talking they will be heard.
6.8: Therefore, do not be like them, for your Father knows what you need, before you ask him.
6.9: Therefore pray in this manner: Our Father in heaven, hallowed be thy name.
6.10: Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done, as in heaven so on earth.
6.11: Give us bread for our needs from day to day.
6.12: And forgive us our offences, as we have forgiven our offenders;
6.13: And do not let us enter into temptation, but deliver us from error. Because thine is the kingdom and the power and the glory for ever and ever. Amen.
6.14: For if you forgive men their faults, your Father in heaven will also forgive you.
6.15: But if you do not forgive men, neither will your Father forgive even your faults.
6.16: When you fast, do not look sad like the hypocrites; for they disfigure their faces, so that they may appear to men that they are fasting. Truly I say to you, that they have already received their reward.
6.17: But you, when you fast, wash your face and anoint your head;
6.18: So that it may not appear to men that you are fasting, but to your Father who is in secret; and your Father who sees in secret, he will reward you.
6.19: Do not lay up for yourselves treasures buried in the ground, a place where rust and moth destroy, and where thieves break through and steal.
6.20: But lay up for yourselves a treasure in heaven, where neither rust nor moth destroy, and where thieves do not break through and steal.
6.21: For where your treasure is, there also is your heart.
6.22: The eye is the lamp of the body; if therefore your eye be clear, your whole body is also lighted.
6.23: But if your eye is diseased, your whole body will be dark. If therefore the light that is in you is darkness, how much more will be your darkness.
6.24: No man can serve two masters; for either he will hate the one, and like the other; or he will honor one, and despise the other. You cannot serve God and mammon (wealth).
6.25: For this reason, I say to you, Do not worry for your life, what you will eat, and what you will drink, nor for your body, what you will wear. Behold, is not life much more important than food, and the body than clothing?
6.26: Watch the birds of the sky, for they do not sow, neither do they harvest, nor gather into barns, and yet your Father in heaven feeds them. Are you not much more important than they?
6.27: Who is among you who by worrying can add one cubit to his stature?
6.28: Why do you worry about clothing? Observe the wild flowers, how they grow; they do not get tired out, nor do they spin.
6.29: But I say to you, that not even Solomon with all of his glory was covered like one of them.
6.30: Now if God clothes in such fashion the grass of the field, which today is and tomorrow falls into the fireplace, is he not much more to you, O you of little faith?
6.31: Therefore do not worry or say, What will we eat, or what will we drink, or with what will we be clothed?
6.32: For worldly people seek after all these things. Your Father in heaven knows that all of these things are also necessary for you.
6.33: But you seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness, and all of these things shall be added to you.
6.34: Therefore do not worry for tomorrow; for tomorrow will look after its own. Sufficient for each day, is its own trouble.

7.1: JUDGE not, that you may not be judged.
7.2: For with the same judgment that you judge, you will be judged, and with the same measure with which you measure, it will be measured to you.
7.3: Why do you see the splinter which is in your brother’s eye, and do not feel the beam which is in your own eye?
7.4: Or how can you say to your brother, let me take out the splinter from your eye, and behold there is a cross beam in your own eye?
7.5: O hypocrites, first take out the beam from your own eye, and then you will see clearly to get out the splinter from your brother’s eye.
7.6: Do not give holy things to the dogs; and do not throw your pearls before the swine, for they might tread them with their feet, and then turn and rend you.
7.7: Ask, and it shall be given to you; seek, and you shall find; knock and it shall be opened to you.
7.8: For whoever asks, receives; and he who seeks, finds; and to him who knocks, the door is opened.
7.9: Or who is the man among you, who when his son asks him for bread, why, will he hand him a stone?
7.10: Or if he should ask him for fish, why, will he hand him a snake?
7.11: If therefore you who err, know how to give good gifts to your sons, how much more will your Father in heaven give good things to those who ask him?
7.12: Whatever you wish men to do for you, do likewise also for them; for this is the law and the prophets.
7.13: Enter in through the narrow door, for wide is the door, and broad is the road which carries to destruction, and many are those who travel on it.
7.14: O how narrow is the door, and how difficult is the road which carries to life, and few are those who are found on it.
7.15: Be careful of false prophets who come to you in lamb’s clothing, but within they are ravening wolves.
7.16: You will know them by their fruits. Why, do they gather grapes from thorns, or figs from thistles?
7.17: So every good tree bears good fruits; but a bad tree bears bad fruits.
7.18: A good tree cannot bear bad fruits, neither can a bad tree bear good fruits.
7.19: Any tree which does not bear good fruits will be cut down and cast into the fire.
7.20: Thus by their fruits you will know them.
7.21: It is not everyone who merely says to me, My Lord, my Lord, will enter into the kingdom of heaven, but he who does the will of my Father in heaven.
7.22: A great many will say to me in that day, My Lord, my Lord, did we not prophesy in your name, and in your name cast out devils, and in your name do many wonders?
7.23: Then I will declare to them, I have never known you; keep away from me, O you that work iniquity.
7.24: Therefore whoever hears these words of mine, and does them, he is like a wise man, who built his house upon a rock.
7.25: And the rain came down, and the rivers overflowed, and the winds blew, and they beat upon that house; but it did not fall down, because its foundations were laid upon a rock.
7.26: And whoever hears these words of mine, and does them not, is like a foolish man, who built his house upon sand.
7.27: And the rain came down, and the rivers overflowed, and the winds blew, and they beat upon that house; and it fell down, and its fall was very great.
7.28: And it happened when Jesus finished these words, the crowds were stunned at his teaching.
7.29: For he taught them as one who had the power, and not like their own scribes and Pharisees.

8.1: WHEN he came down from the mountain, large crowds followed him.
8.2: And behold a leper came and worshipped him, and said, My Lord, if you wish, you can cleanse me.
8.3: And Jesus stretched out his hand and touched him, and he said, I do wish, be cleansed. And in that hour his leprosy was cleansed.
8.4: Jesus then said to him, Look here, why are you telling it to men? go first and show yourself to the priests, and offer an offering as Moses has commanded, for a testimonial to them.
8.5: When Jesus entered Capernaum, a centurion approached him, and appealed to him,
8.6: Saying, My Lord, my boy is lying in the house, paralyzed, and suffering greatly.
8.7: Jesus said to him, I will come and heal him.
8.8: The centurion then answered and said, My Lord, I am not good enough that you should enter under the shadow of my roof; but just say a word, and my boy will be healed.
8.9: For I am also a man in government service, and there are soldiers under my command; and I say to this one, Go, and he goes; and to the other, Come, and he comes; and to my servant, Do this, and he does it.
8.10: When Jesus heard it, he was amazed, and he said to those who accompanied him, Truly I say to you, that not even in Israel have I found such faith as this.
8.11: And I say to you, that a great many will come from the east and from the west, and sit down with Abraham and Isaac and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven.
8.12: But the sons of the kingdom will be put out in the outer darkness; there shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth.
8.13: So Jesus said to the centurion, Go, let it be done to you according to your belief. And his boy was healed in that very hour.
8.14: And Jesus came to the Simon’s house, and he saw his mother-in-law laid up and sick with fever.
8.15: And he touched her hand, and the fever left her, and she got up and waited on them.
8.16: Now when evening came, they brought to him a great many lunatics, and he cured them just by a word; and he healed all who were badly afflicted.
8.17: So that what was spoken by the prophet Isaiah, might be fulfilled, who said, He will take our afflictions and bear our sickness.
8.18: When Jesus saw large crowds surrounding him, he gave orders to go to the crossing place.
8.19: And a scribe drew near and said to him, O my teacher, I will follow you wherever you go.
8.20: Jesus said to him, The foxes have holes, and the fowls of the sky a sheltering place, but the Son of man has no place even to lay his head.
8.21: Another of his disciples said to him, My Lord, permit me first to go and bury my father.
8.22: But Jesus said to him, Come after me, and let the dead bury their own dead.
8.23: And when Jesus went up into the boat, his disciples went with him.
8.24: And behold the sea became very rough, so that the boat was almost covered by the waves; but Jesus was asleep.
8.25: And his disciples came near and woke him up, and said to him, Our Lord, save us, we are perishing.
8.26: Jesus said to them, Why are you fearful, O you of little faith? Then he got up and rebuked the wind and the sea, and there was a great calm.
8.27: But the men were surprised, saying, Who is this man, that even the winds and the sea obey him?
8.28: And when Jesus came to the port on the other side, to the country of the Gadarenes, he was met by two lunatics, who were just coming out of the cemetery. They were exceedingly vicious so that noman would dare to pass by that road.
8.29: And they cried aloud saying, What business have we together, Jesus, ôson of Godö? Have you come here to torment us before the time?
8.30: Now there was near by them a large herd of swine feeding.
8.31: And the lunatics kept asking him, saying, If you are going to heal us, permit us to attack # the herd of swine.
8.32: Jesus said to them, Go. And immediately they left and attacked the swine, and the whole herd went straight over the cliff, and fell into the sea, and were drowned in the water.
8.33: And they who fed them ran away and went to the city, and reported everything that happened, and about the lunatics.
8.34: So all the city went out to meet Jesus; and when they saw him, they urged him to depart from their borders.

9.1: SO he went up into the boat, and crossed over and came to his own city.
9.2: And they brought to him a paralytic, lying on a quilt-bed; and Jesus saw their faith, and he said to the paralytic, Have courage, my son; your sins have been forgiven.
9.3: Some of the scribes said among themselves, This man blasphemes.
9.4: But Jesus knew their thoughts; so he said to them, Why do you think evil in your hearts?
9.5: For which is easier to say, Your sins have been forgiven, or to say, Arise and walk?
9.6: But that you might know that the Son of man has authority on earth to forgive sins, then he said to the paralytic, Arise, take up your quilt-bed, and go to your home.
9.7: And he rose up and went to his home.
9.8: But when the crowds saw it, they were frightened, and they glorified God, because he had given such power as this to men.
9.9: And as Jesus passed from that place, he saw a man whose name was Matthew, sitting in the custom house, and he said to him, Follow me; and he got up and went after him.
9.10: And while they were guests in the house, a great many publicans and sinners came, and they sat as guests with Jesus and with his disciples.
9.11: And when the Pharisees saw it, they said to his disciples, Why does your master eat with publicans and sinners?
9.12: But when Jesus heard it, he said to them, Those who are well need no doctor, but those who are seriously sick.
9.13: Go and learn what this means, I want mercy and not sacrifice; for I came not to invite righteous men, but sinners.
9.14: Then the disciples of John came up to him, and said, Why do we and the Pharisees fast a great deal, and your disciples never fast?
9.15: Jesus said to them, Is it possible for those at the wedding feast to fast as long as the bridegroom is with them? But the days are coming, when the bridegroom will be taken from them, and then they will fast.
9.16: No man puts a new patch on an old garment, so as not to weaken that garment, and make the hole larger.
9.17: Neither do they pour new wine into worn out skins, so as not to rend the skins, and spill the wine, and the wine runs out, and the skins are ruined; but they pour new wine into new skins, and both of them are well preserved.
9.18: While he was speaking these things with them, a leader of the synagogue came near and worshipped him; and he said, My daughter has just died, but come and put your hand on her and she will live.
9.19: And Jesus and his disciples rose up and went with him.
9.20: And behold a woman who had had the hemorrhage for twelve years, came up from behind him, and she touched the edge of his cloak;
9.21: For she was saying to herself, If I can only touch his garment, I will be healed.
9.22: And Jesus turned around and saw her and said to her, Have courage, my daughter, your faith has healed you; and the woman was healed in that very hour.
9.23: So Jesus arrived at the house of the synagogue leader, and saw the singers and the excited crowds.
9.24: And he said to them, That is enough; for the little girl is not dead, but she is asleep; and they laughed at him.
9.25: But when he had put the people out, he went in and held her by her hand, and the little girl got up.
9.26: And this news spread all over that country.
9.27: And as Jesus passed from there, he was delayed by two blind men, who were crying out and saying, Have mercy on us, O son of David.
9.28: And when he came into the house, the same blind men came up to him. Jesus said to them, Do you believe that I can do this? They said to him, Yes, our Lord.
9.29: Then he touched their eyes and said, Let it be to you according to your faith.
9.30: And immediately their eyes were opened; and Jesus charged them and said, See that no one knows it.
9.31: But they went out and spread the news all over that country.
9.32: And when Jesus went out, they brought to him a dumb man who was demented.
9.33: And as soon as he was restored, the dumb man spoke, and the people were amazed and said, Such a thing has never been seen in Israel.
9.34: But the Pharisees said, He is casting out devils by the help of the prince of devils.
9.35: And Jesus travelled in all the cities and villages, teaching in their synagogues, and preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing every kind of sickness and disease.
9.36: When Jesus saw the multitudes, he had compassion on them, because they were tired out and scattered, like sheep which have no shepherd.
9.37: So he said to his disciples, The harvest is great, and the laborers are few;
9.38: Therefore urge the owner of the harvest to bring more laborers to his harvest.

10.1: AND he called his twelve disciples, and gave them power over the unclean spirits, to cast them out, and to heal every kind of disease and sickness.
10.2: The names of the twelve apostles are these: The first of them Simon who is called Peter, and Andrew his brother; James the son of Zebedee, and John his brother;
10.3: Philip and Bartholomew, Thomas and Matthew the publican, James the son of Alphaeus, and Lebbaeus surnamed Thaddaeus;
10.4: Simon the Zealot, and Judas of Iscariot, who betrayed him.
10.5: These twelve Jesus sent out, and charged them and said, Keep away from pagan practices, and do not enter a Samaritan city;
10.6: But above all, go to the sheep which are lost from the house of Israel.
10.7: And as you go, preach and say, that the kingdom of heaven is near.
10.8: Heal the sick, cleanse the lepers, cast out demons; freely you have received, freely give.
10.9: Do not accumulate gold, nor silver, nor brass in your purses;
10.10: Nor a bag for the journey, nor two shirts and shoes, nor a staff; for a laborer is at least worthy of his food.
10.11: Whatever city or town you enter, ask who is trustworthy in it, and remain there until you leave.
10.12: And when you enter into the house, salute the family.
10.13: And if the family is trustworthy, your salutation of peace shall come upon it; but if it is not trustworthy, your salutation shall return to you.
10.14: Whoever will not welcome you, and will not listen to your words, when you leave the house or the village, shake off the sand from your feet.
10.15: Truly I say to you, that it will be easier for the land of Sodom and Gomorrah on the day of judgment than for that city.
10.16: Behold, I am sending you like lambs among the wolves; therefore be wise as serpents, and pure as doves.
10.17: But be careful of men; for they will deliver you up to the courts, and they will scourge you in their synagogues;
10.18: And they will bring you before the presence of governors and kings for my sake, as a testimony to them and to the Gentiles.
10.19: But when they deliver you up, do not worry as to how or what you will speak; for it will be given to you in that very hour what you are to speak.
10.20: For it is not you who speak, but the Spirit of your Father, which speaks through you.
10.21: Brother will deliver up his own brother to death, and father his son; and children will rise up against their parents and put them to death.
10.22: And you will be hated by everybody because of my name; but he who endures until the end shall live.
10.23: When they persecute you in this city, escape to another; for truly I say to you, that you shall not finish converting all the cities of the house of Israel, until the Son of man returns.
10.24: No disciple is more important than his teacher, and no servant than his master.
10.25: It is enough for a disciple to be like his teacher, and for a servant to be like his master. If then, they have called the master of the house Beelzebub, how much more those of his household.
10.26: Therefore do not be afraid of them; for there is nothing covered that will not be uncovered, and hidden that will not be known.
10.27: What I tell you in the dark, tell it in the daylight; and what you hear with your ears, preach on the house tops.
10.28: Do not be afraid of those who kill the body, but who cannot kill the soul; but above all, be afraid of him who can destroy both the soul and the body in hell.
10.29: Are not two sparrows sold for a penny? And yet not one of them will fall on the ground without your Father’s will.
10.30: But so far as you are concerned, even the hairs of your head are all numbered.
10.31: Therefore fear not; you are much more important than many sparrows.
10.32: Everyone therefore who will acknowledge me before men, I will also acknowledge him before my Father in heaven.
10.33: But whoever will deny me before men, I will also deny him before my Father in heaven.
10.34: Do not expect that I have come to bring peace on earth; I have not come to bring peace but a sword.
10.35: For I have come to set a man against his father, and a daughter against her mother, and a daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law.
10.36: And a man’s enemies will be the members of his own household.
10.37: Whoever loves father or mother more than me is not worthy of me; and whoever loves son or daughter more than me is not worthy of me.
10.38: And whoever does not take up his cross and follow me is not worthy of me.
10.39: He who is concerned about his life shall lose it; and he who loses his life for my sake shall find it.
10.40: Whoever receives you, receives me; and whoever receives me, receives him who sent me.
10.41: He who receives a prophet in the name of a prophet, shall receive a prophet’s reward; and whoever receives a righteous man in the name of a righteous man, shall receive a righteous man’s reward.
10.42: Anyone who gives a drink to one of these little ones, if only a cup of cold water, in the name of a disciple, truly I say to you, he shall never lose his reward.

11.1: WHEN Jesus had finished commanding his twelve disciples, he departed from that place to teach and to preach in their cities.
11.2: But when John heard in prison of the works of Christ, he sent by his disciples,
11.3: And said to him, Are you the one who is to come, or are we to expect another?
11.4: Jesus answered and said, Go and describe to John the things which you see and hear.
11.5: The blind see, and the lame walk, and the lepers are cleansed, and the deaf hear, and the dead rise up, and the poor are given hope.
11.6: And blessed is he who does not stumble on account of me.
11.7: When they went away, Jesus began to speak to the people concerning John, What did you go out to the wilderness to see? A reed which is shaken by the wind?
11.8: If not so, what did you go out to see? A man dressed in fine clothes? Behold those who wear fine clothes are in kings’ houses.
11.9: And if not so, What then did you go out to see? A prophet? Yes, I tell you, and much more than a prophet.
11.10: For this is he of whom it is written, Behold, I send my messenger before your face, to prepare the way before you.
11.11: Truly I say to you, that among those who are born of women, there has never risen one who is greater than John the Baptist; and yet even the least person in the kingdom of heaven is greater than he.
11.12: From the days of John the Baptist until now, the kingdom of heaven has been administered by force, and only those in power control it.
11.13: For all the prophets and the law prophesied until John.
11.14: And if you wish, accept it, that he is Elijah who was to come.
11.15: He who has ears to hear, let him hear.
11.16: But to whom shall I liken this generation? It is like boys who sit in the street and call to their friends.
11.17: And say, We have sung to you, but you would not dance; and we have wailed to you but you did not mourn.
11.18: For John came, neither eating nor drinking, and they said he is crazy.
11.19: The Son of man came, eating and drinking, and they said, Behold, a glutton and a wine-bibber, and a friend of publicans and sinners. And yet wisdom is justified by its works.
11.20: Then Jesus began to reproach the cities in which his many works were done, and which did not repent. And he said,
11.21: Woe to you, Chorazin! woe to you, Bethsaida! for if in Tyre and Sidon had been done the works which were done in you, they might have repented in sackcloth and ashes.
11.22: But I say to you, It will be easier for Tyre and Sidon in the day of judgment, than for you.
11.23: And you, Capernaum, which have exalted yourself up to heaven, shall be brought down to Sheol; for if in Sodom had been done the works which were done in you, it would be standing to this day.
11.24: But I say to you, It will be easier for the land of Sodom in the judgment day, than for you.
11.25: At that time, Jesus answered and said, I thank you, O my Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you have hidden these things from the wise and the men of understanding, and you have revealed them to children.
11.26: O yes, my Father, for such was your will.
11.27: Everything has been delivered to me by my Father, and no man knows the Son except the Father, nor does any man know the Father but the Son, and he to whomever the Son wishes to reveal.
11.28: Come to me, all you who are tired out and carrying burdens, and I will give you rest.
11.29: Take my yoke upon you, and learn from me, for I am genial and meek in my heart, and you will find rest to your souls.
11.30: For my yoke is very pleasant, and my burden is light.

12.1: AT that time, Jesus walked on the sabbath through the wheat fields; and his disciples became hungry, and they began to pluck ears of wheat and eat.
12.2: But when the Pharisees saw them, they said to him, Behold, your disciples are doing what is unlawful to do on the sabbath.
12.3: But he said to them, Have you not read what David did, when he and those who were with him were hungry?
12.4: How he entered into the house of God, and did eat bread that was on the table of the Lord, that which was not lawful for him to eat, nor for those who were with him, but only for the priests?
12.5: Or, have you not read in the book of law, that the priests in the temple disregard the sabbath, and yet are blameless?
12.6: But I say to you, that there is one here, who is greater than the temple.
12.7: But if you only knew what it means, I want mercy and not sacrifice, you would not condemn those who are blameless.
12.8: For the Son of man is Lord of the sabbath.
12.9: And Jesus departed from thence and came to their synagogue.
12.10: And there was a man there whose hand was withered. And they questioned him, saying, Is it lawful to heal on the sabbath? that they might accuse him.
12.11: He said to them, Who is the man among you who has only one sheep, and if it should fall into a pit on the sabbath, would he not take hold of it and lift it up?
12.12: How much more important is a man than a sheep? It is therefore lawful to do good on the sabbath.
12.13: Then he said to the man, Stretch out your hand. And he stretched out his hand, and it was restored like the other.
12.14: And the Pharisees went out, and they took counsel concerning him, so as to do away with him.
12.15: But Jesus knew of it, and departed from thence; and a great many people followed him, and he healed them all.
12.16: And he charged them not to say where he was,
12.17: So that what was said by the prophet Isaiah might be fulfilled, who said,
12.18: Behold my servant with whom I am pleased, my beloved one, in whom my soul rejoices; I will put my Spirit upon him, and he will preach justice to the peoples.
12.19: He will not argue, nor will he cry aloud; and no man will hear his voice in the street.
12.20: He will not break even a bruised reed, and he will not extinguish a flickering lamp, until he brings justice to victory;
12.21: And in his name will the peoples find hope.
12.22: Then they brought near to him a lunatic, who was also dumb and blind; and he healed him, so that the dumb and blind man could speak and see.
12.23: All the people were amazed and said, Perhaps this man is the son of David?
12.24: But when the Pharisees heard of it, they said, This man does not cast out demons, except by Beelzebub, the prince of demons.
12.25: But Jesus knew their thoughts, and said to them, Every kingdom which is divided against itself, will be destroyed; and every house or city that is divided against itself, will not stand.
12.26: And if Satan cast out Satan. he is divided against himself; how then will his kingdom stand?
12.27: So if I cast out demons by Beelzebub, by what do your sons cast them out? for this reason they will be your judges.
12.28: And if I cast out devils by the Spirit of God, then the kingdom of God has come near to you.
12.29: Or, how can a man enter into a strong man’s house and plunder his goods, except he first bind the strong man, and then he plunders his house?
12.30: He who is not with me is against me; and he who does not gather with me, shall be dispersed.
12.31: Therefore I say to you, that all sins and blasphemies will be forgiven to men; but the blasphemy against the Spirit shall not be forgiven to men.
12.32: And whoever speaks a word against the Son of man, will be forgiven; but whoever speaks against the Holy Spirit shall not be forgiven, neither in this world nor in the world to come.
12.33: Either produce like a good tree with good fruits, or produce like a bad tree with bad fruits; for a tree is known by its fruits.
12.34: O generation of scorpions, how can you speak good things when you are bad? For the mouth speaks from the fullness of the heart.
12.35: A good man brings out good things from good treasures, and a bad man brings out bad things from bad treasures.
12.36: For I say to you, that for every foolish word which men speak, they will have to answer for it on the day of judgment.
12.37: For by your words you shall be justified, and by your words you shall be found guilty.
12.38: Then some of the men of the scribes and Pharisees answered and said to him, Teacher, we would like to see a sign from you.
12.39: But he answered and said to them, An evil and adulterous generation wants a sign; and no sign will be given to it, except the sign of the prophet Jonah.
12.40: For as Jonah was in the whale’s belly three days and three nights, so the Son of man will be in the heart of the earth, three days and three nights.
12.41: Even the men of Nineveh will rise up in judgment with this generation, and find it guilty; for they repented through the preaching of Jonah, and behold, a greater than Jonah is here.
12.42: The queen of the south will rise up in judgment with this generation, and find it guilty; for she came from the far ends of the earth that she might hear Solomon’s wisdom, and behold a greater than Solomon is here.
12.43: When an unclean spirit goes out of a man, it travels in places where there is no water, and seeks rest, and does not find it.
12.44: Then it says, I will return to my own house from whence I came out; so it comes back and finds it empty, warm, and well furnished.
12.45: Then it goes away and brings with it seven other spirits worse than itself, and they enter and live in it; and the end of that man becomes worse than at first. Such will happen to this evil generation.
12.46: While he was speaking to the people, his mother and his brothers came and stood outside, and wanted to speak with him.
12.47: Then a man said to him, Behold your mother and your brothers are standing outside, and they want to speak with you.
12.48: But he answered and said to him who told him, Who is my mother and who are my brothers?
12.49: And he pointed his hand to his disciples and said, Behold my mother, and behold my brothers.
12.50: For whoever does the will of my Father in heaven, he is my brother and my sister and my mother.

13.1: THAT same day Jesus went out of the house, and sat by the seaside.
13.2: And many people gathered around him, so that he had to go up and sit in a boat, and all the people stood on the seashore.
13.3: And he spoke many things to them in parables, and said, Behold the sower went out to sow;
13.4: And when he had sown, some seed fell on the roadside, and the fowls came and ate it.
13.5: Other fell upon the rock, where there was not sufficient soil; and it sprang up earlier because the ground was not deep enough;
13.6: But when the sun shone, it was scorched, and because it had no root, it dried up;
13.7: And other fell among thistles, and the thistles sprung up and choked it.
13.8: And other fell in good soil, and bore fruit, some one hundred, and some sixty, and some thirty.
13.9: He who has ears to hear, let him hear.
13.10: Then his disciples drew near to him and said, Why do you speak to them in parables?
13.11: He answered and said to them, Because to you it is granted to know the mystery of the kingdom of heaven, but it is not granted to them.
13.12: For to him who has, shall be given and it shall increase to him; but to him who has not, even that which he has shall be taken away from him.
13.13: This is the reason I speak to them in figures, because they see and yet cannot perceive; and they hear and yet do not listen, nor do they understand.
13.14: And in them is fulfilled the prophecy of Isaiah who said, Hearing you will hear, but you will not understand; and seeing you will see, but you will not know.
13.15: For the heart of this people has become hardened, and their ears hear heavily, and their eyes are dull; so that they cannot see with their eyes, and hear with their ears, and understand with their hearts; let them return, and I will heal them.
13.16: But as for you, blessed are your eyes for they see; and your ears for they hear.
13.17: For truly I say to you, a great many prophets and righteous men have longed to see what you see, and did not see it; and to hear what you hear, and did not hear it.
13.18: Now you listen to the parable of the seed.
13.19: Whoever hears the word of the kingdom and does not understand it, the evil one comes and snatches away the word which has been sown in his heart. This is that which was sown on the roadside.
13.20: That which was sown upon the rock, this is he who hears the word, and immediately accepts it with joy;
13.21: But it has no root in him, except for a while; and when trouble or persecution comes because of the word, he immediately stumbles.
13.22: That which was sown among thistles, this is he who hears the word, but worldly thoughts and the deception caused by riches choke the word, and it becomes fruitless.
13.23: That which was sown upon good soil, this is he who hears my word, and understands it, so he bears fruit and produces some one hundred, and some sixty, and some thirty.
13.24: He related another parable to them, and said, The kingdom of heaven is like a man who sowed good seed in his field.
13.25: And when the men slept, his enemy came and sowed tares among the wheat, and went away.
13.26: But when the blade sprang up and bore fruit, then the tares also appeared.
13.27: So the servants of the landowner came and said to him, Our lord, behold, did you not sow good seed in your field; whence did the tares come into it?
13.28: He said to them, An enemy did this; his servants then said to him, Do you want us to go and pick them out?
13.29: But he said to them, It might happen that while you were picking out the tares, you might uproot with them also the wheat.
13.30: Let them both grow together until the harvest; and at the harvest season, I will say to the reapers, Pick out first the tares, and bind them into bundles to be burned; but gather the wheat into my barns.
13.31: He related another parable to them, and said, The kingdom of heaven is like a grain of mustard seed, which a man took and sowed in his field.
13.32: It is the smallest of all seeds; but when it is grown, it is larger than all of the herbs; and it becomes a tree, so that the fowls of the sky come and nest in its branches.
13.33: He told them another parable. The kingdom of heaven is like the leaven, which a woman took and buried in three measures of flour, until it was all leavened.
13.34: Jesus spoke all these things to the people in parables; and without parables he did not speak to them.
13.35: So that it might be fulfilled which was said by the prophet, who said, I will open my mouth in parables, and I will bring out secrets hidden before the foundation of the world.
13.36: Then Jesus left the multitudes and came into the house; and his disciples came up to him, and said, Explain to us the parable of the tares and the field.
13.37: He answered and said to them, He who sowed good seed is the Son of man.
13.38: The field is the world; the good seed are the sons of the kingdom; but the tares are the sons of evil.
13.39: The enemy who sowed them is Satan; the harvest is the end of the world; and the reapers are the angels.
13.40: Therefore, just as the tares are picked out and burned in the fire, so shall it be at the end of the world.
13.41: The Son of man will send his angels, and they will pick out from his kingdom all things which cause stumbling, and all workers of iniquity.
13.42: And they will throw them into the furnace of fire; there shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth.
13.43: Then the righteous ones shall shine as the sun in the kingdom of their Father. He who has ears to hear, let him hear.
13.44: Again, the kingdom of heaven is like a treasure which is hidden in the field, which a man discovered and hid, and because of his joy, he went and sold everything he had, and bought that field.
13.45: Again, the kingdom of heaven is like a merchant, who was seeking good pearls.
13.46: And when he had found one costly pearl, he went and sold everything he had, and bought it.
13.47: Again, the kingdom of heaven is like a net which was thrown into the sea, and it gathered fish of every kind.
13.48: When it was filled, they drew it to the shore, and sat down and sorted them; the good ones they put into bags, and the bad they threw away.
13.49: So will it be at the end of the world; the angels will go out and separate the bad from among the righteous,
13.50: And they will throw them into the furnace of fire; there shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth.
13.51: Jesus said to them, Have you understood all of these things? They said to him, Yes, our Lord.
13.52: He said to them, Therefore every scribe who is converted to the kingdom of heaven, is like a man who is a householder, who brings out new and old things from his treasures.
13.53: When Jesus had finished these parables, he departed thence.
13.54: And he came to his own city; and he taught them in their synagogues, in such a way, that they were amazed and said, Where did he get this wisdom and these wonders?
13.55: Is he not the carpenter’s son? Is not his mother called Mary? and his brothers, James and Joses and Simon and Judah?
13.56: Are not all his sisters with us? Where did he get all these things?
13.57: And they were perplexed about him. But Jesus said to them, No prophet is insulted, except in his own city and in his own house.
13.58: And he did not perform many miracles there, because of their unbelief.

14.1: AT that time Herod the tetrarch heard the news about Jesus.
14.2: And he said to his servants, This man is John the Baptist; he has risen from the dead; this is why great miracles are wrought by him.
14.3: For Herod had arrested John, and bound him, and put him in prison, because of Herodias, his brother Philip’s wife.
14.4: For John had said to him, It is unlawful to have her as your wife.
14.5: So Herod wanted to kill him, but he was afraid of the people, because they accepted him as a prophet.
14.6: When Herod’s birthday came, the daughter of Herodias danced before the guests, and it pleased Herod.
14.7: He therefore swore to her with oaths, that he would give her anything that she asked.
14.8: And she, because she was instructed by her mother, said, Give me right here on a tray the head of John the Baptist.
14.9: And the king was very sorry; but because of the oaths and the guests, he commanded that it be given to her.
14.10: So he sent and had John beheaded in the prison.
14.11: And his head was brought in on a tray, and given to the girl; and she took it to her mother.
14.12: Then his disciples came and took up his body and buried it, and they came and informed Jesus.
14.13: When Jesus heard it, he departed thence by boat, alone to a desert place; and when the people heard of it, they followed him by land from the cities.
14.14: And Jesus went out and saw large crowds, and he had pity for them, and healed their sick.
14.15: When it was evening, his disciples came to him, and they said to him, This is a lonely place, and it is getting late; dismiss the people so that the men may go to the villages and buy food for themselves.
14.16: But he said to them, It is not necessary for them to go; you give them something to eat.
14.17: They said to him, We have nothing here, except five loaves of bread and two fish.
14.18: Jesus said to them, Bring them here to me.
14.19: And he ordered the people to sit down on the ground, and he took the five loaves of bread and the two fish, and he looked up to heaven and he blessed them, and he broke them, and gave them to his disciples, and the disciples placed them before the people.
14.20: So they all ate, and were satisfied; and they took up the fragments which were left over, twelve full baskets.
14.21: And the men who ate were five thousand, not counting the women and children.
14.22: And immediately he urged his disciples to go up into the boat, in advance of him to the crossing place, while he dismissed the people.
14.23: And when he had dismissed the people, he went up to the mountain alone to pray; and when darkness fell he was still there alone.
14.24: But the boat was many miles away from the land, tossed by the waves, for the wind was against it.
14.25: And in the fourth watch of the night, Jesus came to them, walking on the water.
14.26: And his disciples saw him walking on the water, and they were scared, and they said, It is a false vision; and they cried out because of their fear.
14.27: But Jesus spoke to them at once and said, Have courage; it is I; do not be afraid.
14.28: And Peter answered and said to him, My Lord, if it is you, command me to come to you on the water.
14.29: Jesus said to him, Come. So Peter went down from the boat, and walked on the water, to come to Jesus.
14.30: But when he saw that the wind was severe, he was afraid, and began to sink, and he raised his voice and said, My Lord, save me.
14.31: And our Lord immediately stretched out his hand and grasped him; and he said to him, O you of little faith, why did you doubt?
14.32: And when they went up into the boat, the wind quieted down.
14.33: And they who were in the boat came and worshipped him; and they said, Truly you are the Son of God.
14.34: And they rowed and came to the land of Gennesaret.
14.35: And the men of that country recognized him, and they sent word to all the villages around them; so they brought to him all who were seriously sick.
14.36: And they besought him, that they might touch even the edge of his robe; and those who touched it were healed.

15.1: THEN Pharisees and scribes from Jerusalem came up to Jesus, saying,
15.2: Why do your disciples disregard the tradition of the elders, and they do not wash their hands when they eat food?
15.3: Jesus answered and said to them, Why do you also disregard the commandment of God on account of your tradition.
15.4: For God said, Honor your father and your mother, and whoever curses his father and his mother, let him be put to death.
15.5: But you say, Whoever says to a father or to a mother, Whatever you may be benefited from me is Corban (my offering), he need not honor his father or his mother.
15.6: So you have rendered useless the word of God for the sake of your tradition.
15.7: O you hypocrites, the prophet Isaiah well prophesied concerning you and said,
15.8: This people honor me with their lips, but their heart is far away from me.
15.9: And they worship me in vain, when they teach the doctrines of the commandments of men.
15.10: Then he called the people and said to them, Listen and understand.
15.11: It is not what enters into the mouth which defiles man; but what goes out of the mouth, that is what defiles man.
15.12: Then his disciples came up and said to him, Do you know that the Pharisees who heard this saying were offended?
15.13: But he answered and said to them, Every plant that my heavenly Father did not plant, shall be uprooted.
15.14: Leave them alone; they are blind guides of the blind. And if the blind lead around the blind, both will fall into a pit.
15.15: And Simon Peter answered and said to him, My Lord, explain this parable to us.
15.16: And he said to them, Even yet do you not understand?
15.17: Do you not know that what enters into the mouth goes into the stomach, and thence, through the intestines, is cast out?
15.18: But what comes out of the mouth comes out from the heart; and that is what defiles man.
15.19: For from the heart come out evil thoughts, such as fornication, murder, adultery, theft, false witness, blasphemy.
15.20: It is these that defile man; but if a man should eat when his hands are unwashed, he will not be defiled.
15.21: And Jesus went out from thence, and he came to the border of Tyre and Sidon.
15.22: And behold, a Canaanite woman from these borders, came out crying aloud, and saying, Have mercy on me, O my Lord, son of David; my daughter is seriously afflicted with insanity.
15.23: But he did not answer her. And his disciples came up to him and urged him, saying, Dismiss her, for she keeps crying aloud after us.
15.24: And he answered and said to them, I am not sent, except to the sheep which went astray from the house of Israel.
15.25: But she came and worshipped him, and said, My Lord, help me.
15.26: Jesus said to her, It is not right to take the children’s bread and throw it to the dogs.
15.27: But she said Yes, my Lord, even the dogs eat of the crumbs which fall from the master’s tray, and they live.
15.28: Then Jesus said to her, O woman, your faith is great; let it be to you as you wish; and her daughter was healed from that very hour.
15.29: And Jesus departed from thence, and he came toward the sea of Galilee; and he went up to a mountain and sat down there.
15.30: And a great many people came to him, who had with them the lame, blind, dumb, maimed, and many others; and they laid them down at the feet of Jesus, and he healed them.
15.31: So that the people wondered, to see the dumb speaking, and the maimed healed, and the lame walking, and the blind seeing; and they praised the God of Israel.
15.32: Jesus then called his disciples and said to them, I have compassion for this people, for they have remained with me three days, and they have nothing to eat; and if I dismiss them fasting, they might faint on the way; but this I do not wish to do.
15.33: His disciples said to him, Where can we get bread in this desolate place to feed all this people?
15.34: Jesus said to them, How many loaves of bread have you? They said to him, Seven, and a few small fish.
15.35: So he ordered the people to sit on the ground.
15.36: Then he took the seven loaves of bread and the fish, and gave thanks, and he broke them, and gave to his disciples, and the disciples gave them to the people.
15.37: And all of them did eat and were satisfied; and they took up of the fragments that were left over, seven full baskets.
15.38: And those who did eat were four thousand men, besides women and children.
15.39: And when he had dismissed the people, he went up to the boat and came to the border of Magadan.

16.1: AND the Pharisees and Sadducees came up to him to tempt him; and they asked him to show them a sign from heaven.
16.2: But he answered and said to them, When it is evening, you say, It is clear, for the sky is red.
16.3: And in the morning you say, It is a winter day, for the sky is red and cloudy. O hypocrites, you know how to judge the face of the sky, but the signs of the present time you are not able to distinguish.
16.4: A wicked and adulterous generation wants a sign; and no sign shall be given to it, except the sign of the prophet Jonah. And he left them and went away.
16.5: When his disciples came to the crossing place, they had forgotten to take bread with them.
16.6: He said to them, Look out and beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and of the Sadducees.
16.7: And they were reasoning among themselves and saying, It is because we have not brought bread.
16.8: But Jesus knew it and said to them, What are you thinking among yourselves, O you of little faith; is it because you have not brought bread?
16.9: Do you not yet understand? Do you not remember the five loaves of bread of the five thousand, and how many baskets you took up?
16.10: Neither the seven loaves of bread of the four thousand, and how many baskets you took up?
16.11: How is it that you did not understand that I was not talking to you about the bread, but to beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and of the Sadducees?
16.12: Then they understood, that he did not say that they should beware of the leaven of the bread, but of the teaching of the Pharisees and the Sadducees.
16.13: When Jesus came to the country of Caesarea of Philippi, he asked his disciples saying, What do the men say concerning me, that I am merely a son of man?
16.14: They said, There are some who say John the Baptist, others Elijah, and still others Jeremiah, or one of the prophets:
16.15: He said to them, Who do you say that I am?
16.16: Simon Peter answered and said, You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.
16.17: Jesus answered and said to him, Blessed are you, Simon son of Jonah, for flesh and blood did not reveal it to you, but my Father in heaven.
16.18: I tell you also that you are a stone, and upon this stone I will build my church; and the doors of Sheol shall not shut in on it.
16.19: I will give you the keys of the kingdom of heaven; and whatever you bind on earth shall be bound in heaven, and whatever you release on earth shall be released in heaven.
16.20: Then he charged his disciples not to tell any man that he is the Christ.
16.21: From that time Jesus began to make known to his disciples, that he will shortly have to go to Jerusalem, and suffer a great deal from the elders, and the high priests and scribes, and be killed, and rise up on the third day.
16.22: So Peter took him aside and began to rebuke him, and he said, Far be it from you, my Lord, that this should happen to you.
16.23: But he turned, and said to Peter, Get behind me, Satan, you are a stumbling-block to me; for you are not thinking of the things of God, but of men.
16.24: Then Jesus said to his disciples, He who wishes to come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross and follow me.
16.25: For whoever wishes to save his life shall lose it; and whoever loses his life for my sake shall find it.
16.26: For how will a man be benefited, if he should gain the whole world and lose his own soul? Or what shall a man give in exchange for his soul?
16.27: For the Son of man will come in the glory of his Father with his holy angels; and then he will reward each man according to his works.
16.28: Truly I say to you, There are men who stand here, who will not taste death, until they see the Son of man coming in his kingdom.

17.1: AND after six days Jesus took Peter and James and his brother John, and brought them up to a high mountain alone.
17.2: And Jesus was transfigured before them, and his face shone like the sun, and his clothes turned white like light.
17.3: And there appeared to them Moses and Elijah, as they were talking with him.
17.4: Then Peter answered and said to Jesus, My Lord, it is better for us to remain here; and if you wish, we will make three shelters here, one for you, and one for Moses, and one for Elijah.
17.5: And while he was speaking, behold, a bright cloud overshadowed them, and a voice came out of the cloud saying, This is my beloved Son, I am pleased with him; hear him.
17.6: When the disciples heard it, they threw themselves on their faces, and they were greatly frightened.
17.7: And Jesus came near them and touched them, and said, Arise, do not be afraid.
17.8: And they raised up their eyes, and they saw no man, except Jesus alone.
17.9: And as they were going down from the mountain, Jesus commanded them, and said to them, Do not speak of this vision in the presence of anyone, until the Son of man rises from the dead.
17.10: And his disciples asked him, and said, Why then do the scribes say that Elijah must come first?
17.11: Jesus answered and said to them, Elijah will come first, so that everything might be fulfilled.
17.12: But I say to you, Elijah has already come, and they did not know him, and they did to him whatever they pleased. Thus also the Son of man is bound to suffer from them.
17.13: Then the disciples understood that what he had told them was about John the Baptist.
17.14: And when they came to the people, a man approached him and knelt on his knees, and said to him,
17.15: My Lord, have mercy on me; my son is an epileptic and has become worse; he often falls into the fire, and often into the water.
17.16: And I brought him to your disciples, but they were not able to heal him.
17.17: Jesus answered and said, O faithless and crooked generation, how long shall I be with you? and how long shall I preach to you? bring him here to me.
17.18: And Jesus rebuked him, and the demon went out of him; and the boy was healed from that very hour.
17.19: Then the disciples came up to Jesus when he was alone, and said to him, Why could we not heal him?
17.20: Jesus said to them, Because of your unbelief; for truly I say to you, If there is faith in you even as a grain of mustard, you will say to this mountain, move away from here, and it will move away; and nothing would prevail over you.
17.21: Nevertheless this kind does not come out, except by fasting and prayer.
17.22: While they were returning through Galilee, Jesus said to them, The Son of man will shortly be delivered into the hands of men;
17.23: And they will kill him, and on the third day he will rise up. And they were very much grieved.
17.24: And when they came to Capernaum, those who collect two coins of silver as headtax came to Peter and said to him, Would not your master give his two coins?
17.25: He said to them, Yes. And when Peter entered the house, Jesus anticipated and said to him, What do you think, Simon? from whom do the kings of the earth collect custom duties and head- tax? from their sons, or from strangers?
17.26: Simon said to him, From strangers. Jesus said to him, Then the sons are free.
17.27: But so as not to offend them, go to the sea, and throw out a hook, and the first fish which comes up, open its mouth and you will find a coin; take it and give it for me and for you.

18.1: AT that very hour the disciples came up to Jesus and said, Who is greatest in the kingdom of heaven?
18.2: So Jesus called a little boy, and made him to stand up in the midst of them,
18.3: And he said, Truly I say to you, Unless you change and become like little boys, you shall not enter into the kingdom of heaven.
18.4: Whoever therefore will humble himself like this little boy, shall be great in the kingdom of heaven.
18.5: And he who will welcome one like this little boy, in my name, welcomes me.
18.6: And whoever misleads one of these little ones who believe in me, it would be better for him that an ass’ millstone were hanged on his neck and he were sunk in the depths of the sea.
18.7: Woe to the world because of offences! Offences are bound to come; but woe to the man by whose hand the offences come!
18.8: If your hand or your foot offends you, cut it off and throw it away from you; for it is much better for you to go through life lamed or maimed, rather than having two hands or two feet, and fall into the everlasting fire.
18.9: And if your eye offends you, remove it and throw it away from you; it is better for you to go through life with one eye, rather than having two eyes and fall into the gehenna of fire.
18.10: See to it that you do not despise one of these little ones; for I say to you, their angels always see the face of my Father in heaven.
18.11: For the Son of man has come to save what was lost.
18.12: What do you think? If a man should have a hundred sheep, and one of them is lost, would he not leave the ninety and nine on the mountain, and go in search of the one which is lost?
18.13: And if he should find it, truly I say to you, he rejoices over it more than over the ninety and nine which were not lost.
18.14: Even so, your Father in heaven does not want one of these little ones to be lost.
18.15: Now then, if your brother is at fault with you, go and rebuke him alone; if he listens to you, then you have won your brother.
18.16: But if he will not listen to you, take one or two with you, because at the mouth of two or three witnesses every word is sustained.
18.17: And if he will not listen to them, tell the congregation; and if he will not listen to the congregation, then regard him as a publican and a heathen.
18.18: Truly I say to you, Whatever you bind on earth will be bound in heaven, and whatever you release on earth will be released in heaven.
18.19: Again I say to you, that if two of you are worthy on earth, anything that they would ask, it will be done for them by my Father in heaven.
18.20: For wherever two or three are gathered in my name, I am there among them.
18.21: Then Peter came up and said to him, My Lord, if my brother is at fault with me, how many times should I forgive him? up to seven times?
18.22: Jesus said to him, I do not say to you up to seven times, but up to seventy times seven.
18.23: Therefore the kingdom of heaven is likened to a king who wanted to take an accounting from his servants.
18.24: And when he began to take the accounting, they brought to him one who owed ten thousand talents.
18.25: And as he could not pay, his lord commanded him to be sold, together with his wife and children, and all that he had, so that he could pay.
18.26: The servant then fell down, worshipped him, and said, My lord, have patience with me, and I will pay you everything.
18.27: Then the master of that servant had pity, so he released him, and cancelled his debt.
18.28: But that servant went out, and found one of his fellow-servants, who owed him one hundred cents; and he seized him, and tried to choke him, saying to him, Give me what you owe me.
18.29: So his fellow-servant fell down at his feet, and begged him and said, Have patience with me, and I will pay you.
18.30: But he was not willing; and he went and had him put into prison, until he should pay him what he owed him.
18.31: When their fellow-servants saw what had happened, they were very sorry, and they came and informed their master of everything that had happened.
18.32: Then his master called him and said to him, O wicked servant, I cancelled all your debt because you begged me.
18.33: Was it not right for you to have mercy on your fellow-servant, just as I had mercy on you?
18.34: So his master was angry, and delivered him to the scourgers, until he should pay everything he owed him.
18.35: So will my Father in heaven do to you, if you do not forgive each man his brother’s fault from your heart.

19.1: WHEN Jesus had finished these sayings, he departed from Galilee, and came to the border of Judaea, at the crossing of the Jordan.
19.2: And a great many people followed him, and he healed them there.
19.3: And the Pharisees came up to him and were tempting him and saying, Is it lawful for a man to divorce his wife for any cause?
19.4: But he answered and said to them, Have you not read, that he who made from the beginning, made them male and female?
19.5: And he said, Because of this, a man shall leave his father and his mother, and shall be joined to his wife, and the two shall be one flesh.
19.6: Henceforth they are not two, but one body; therefore what God has joined together, man must not separate.
19.7: They said to him, Why then did Moses command to give a letter of separation and then divorce her?
19.8: He said to them, Moses, considering the hardness of your heart, gave you permission to divorce your wives; but from the beginning it was not so.
19.9: But I say to you, Whoever leaves his wife without a charge of adultery and marries another commits adultery; and he who marries a woman thus separated commits adultery.
19.10: His disciples said to him, If there is so much scandal between man and woman, it is not worthwhile to marry.
19.11: He said to them, This saying does not apply to every man, but to whom it is needed.
19.12: For there are eunuchs who were born this way from their mother’s womb; and there are eunuchs who were made eunuchs by men; and there are eunuchs who made themselves eunuchs for the sake of the kingdom of heaven. To him who can grasp, this is enough.
19.13: Then they brought little boys to him, that he may lay his hand on them and pray; and his disciples rebuked them.
19.14: But Jesus said to them, Allow the little boys to come to me, and do not stop them; for the kingdom of heaven is for such as these.
19.15: And he laid his hand on them, and went away from thence.
19.16: Then a man came up and said to him, O good Teacher, what is the best that I should do to have life eternal?
19.17: He said to him, Why do you call me good? There is no one who is good except the one God; but if you want to enter into life, obey the commandments.
19.18: He said to him, Which ones? And Jesus said to him, You shall not kill; You shall not commit adultery; You shall not steal; You shall not bear false witness;
19.19: Honor your father and your mother; and, Love your neighbor as yourself.
19.20: The young man said to him, I have obeyed all these from my boyhood, what do I lack?
19.21: Jesus said to him. If you wish to be perfect, go and sell your possessions and give them to the poor, and you will have a treasure in heaven; then follow me.
19.22: When the young man heard this word, he went away sad, for he had great possessions.
19.23: Jesus then said to his disciples, Truly I say to you, It is difficult for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of heaven.
19.24: Again I say to you, It is easier for a rope to go through the eye of a needle, than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God.
19.25: When the disciples heard it, they were exceedingly astonished, saying, Who then can be saved?
19.26: Jesus looked at them and said, For men this is impossible, but for God everything is possible.
19.27: Then Peter answered and said to him, Behold, we have left everything and followed you; what will we have?
